title: The temptation of Jesus part 1
scripture_text: Some of what we will cover in this section we have mentioned before. But it is worth repeating.
devotional:
‘’Then Jesus was led by the Spirit into the wilderness to be tempted by the devil. After fasting forty days and forty nights, he was hungry. The tempter came to him and said, “If you are the Son of God, tell these stones to become bread.” Jesus answered, “It is written: ‘Man shall not live on bread alone, but on every word that comes from the mouth of God.’” Then the devil took him to the holy city and had him stand on the highest point of the temple. “If you are the Son of God, he said, “throw yourself down. For it is written: “‘He will command his angels concerning you, and they will lift you up in their hands, so that you will not strike your foot against a stone. Jesus answered him ‘’It is also written: ‘Do not put the Lord your God to the test.’ Again, the devil took him to a very high mountain and showed him all the kingdoms of the world and their splendour “All this I will give you,” he said, “if you will bow down and worship me.” ‘Jesus said to him, “Away from me, Satan! For it is written: ‘Worship the Lord your God, and serve him only.’ Then the devil left him, and angels came and attended him.

In some ways the temptation of Jesus in the wilderness is a revelation of the attitudes and mindset that a child of God should demonstrate.

What does it mean for us to be Children of God?

What does son-ship look like?

How should we as Children of God think and act?

What kind of relationship does God as our father wants to have with us?

The temptation of Jesus is a blue print for son-ship.

Most of the personal issues we face as people are rooted in our lack of understanding our identity as children of God. Often our hearts are flooded with insecurities, fears and anxieties. We struggle to accept who we are, longing to be someone else. For many Christians ‘Child of God’ is more a title than an experience, it is more of a theological concept than a dynamic reality and yet the most important and significant thing about any Christian is that they are a Child of God. This status is more important than their marriage, ministry, vocation, achievements or any other thing they may value highly.

The basis of son-ship is knowing that you are truly loved.

Before we look in depth at the temptation that Jesus faced in the wilderness, we need to look at how His father prepared Jesus for this ordeal, and how the father planned it.

When Jesus was being baptised, a voice spoke from Heaven saying “This is my Son, whom I love; with him I am well pleased.”

If Jesus was going to come through the ordeal of the temptation then he would need to know deep within his heart that he was loved by his father. The love of the father, gave Jesus the security and wisdom he needed to discern and refuse the temptations that Satan would put before him.

What was God the Father communicating, when He spoke to Jesus after His baptism?

The word used for love is agapetos which means to be ultimately concerned for the welfare of the one who is loved. It carries the idea of being loved deeply. Often we can have a superficial response to the deep love of God. We don’t let it penetrate our lives or marinate our hearts. It’s important to note that up to this point in Jesus’ life, He had done no teaching, nor had he performed any miracles; he had just lived a quiet life with his family in Nazareth. Yet he was completely loved by his father in heaven.

As Jesus endured the hunger and desolation of the wilderness he was acutely aware that he was loved.

God not only prepared Jesus to face the gruelling ordeal of the wilderness, but He led him into it.

All three synoptic gospels give us the context of Jesus’ temptation. Matthew says: - “Then Jesus was led by the Spirit into the desert.” In Luke we read, “Jesus full of the Holy Spirit, returned from the Jordan and was led by the Spirit in the desert.” Mark also gives a shorter account of Jesus’ temptation, Mark says “At once the Spirit sent him out into the desert.”

There is a lot in this which we will look at later, but for now notice that Jesus was loved then he was led. That is the way it needs to be. It is very easy for any of us to become stressed or anxious about God’s leading in our lives. We wonder what God wants us to do with our lives, we want some divine plan written out in the sky, but it will never happen like that. Personally, I think it is far more important to know God’s love, than to know God’s plans. Perhaps all the stress and anxiety around the whole area of Gods will for our lives is the result of us not having a deep enough experience of His love.
prayer: Father thank you that it is the knowledge of your love that enables me to face the difficulties of life.
html:
The temptation of Jesus part 1

Study Text: Matthew 4 1-11
Some of what we will cover in this section we have mentioned before. But it is worth repeating.

‘’Then Jesus was led by the Spirit into the wilderness to be tempted by the devil. After fasting forty days and forty nights, he was hungry. The tempter came to him and said, “If you are the Son of God, tell these stones to become bread.” Jesus answered, “It is written: ‘Man shall not live on bread alone, but on every word that comes from the mouth of God.’” Then the devil took him to the holy city and had him stand on the highest point of the temple. “If you are the Son of God, he said, “throw yourself down. For it is written: “‘He will command his angels concerning you, and they will lift you up in their hands, so that you will not strike your foot against a stone. Jesus answered him ‘’It is also written: ‘Do not put the Lord your God to the test.’ Again, the devil took him to a very high mountain and showed him all the kingdoms of the world and their splendour “All this I will give you,” he said, “if you will bow down and worship me.” ‘Jesus said to him, “Away from me, Satan! For it is written: ‘Worship the Lord your God, and serve him only.’ Then the devil left him, and angels came and attended him.

In some ways the temptation of Jesus in the wilderness is a revelation of the attitudes and mindset that a child of God should demonstrate.

What does it mean for us to be Children of God?

What does son-ship look like?

How should we as Children of God think and act?

What kind of relationship does God as our father wants to have with us?

The temptation of Jesus is a blue print for son-ship.

Most of the personal issues we face as people are rooted in our lack of understanding our identity as children of God. Often our hearts are flooded with insecurities, fears and anxieties. We struggle to accept who we are, longing to be someone else. For many Christians ‘Child of God’ is more a title than an experience, it is more of a theological concept than a dynamic reality and yet the most important and significant thing about any Christian is that they are a Child of God. This status is more important than their marriage, ministry, vocation, achievements or any other thing they may value highly.

The basis of son-ship is knowing that you are truly loved.

Before we look in depth at the temptation that Jesus faced in the wilderness, we need to look at how His father prepared Jesus for this ordeal, and how the father planned it.

The word used for love is agapetos which means to be ultimately concerned for the welfare of the one who is loved. It carries the idea of being loved deeply. As Jesus endured the hunger and desolation of the wilderness he was acutely aware that he was loved.

God not only prepared Jesus to face the gruelling ordeal of the wilderness, but He led him into it.

All three synoptic gospels give us the context of Jesus’ temptation. Matthew says: - “Then Jesus was led by the Spirit into the desert.” In Luke we read, “Jesus full of the Holy Spirit, returned from the Jordan and was led by the Spirit in the desert.” Mark also gives a shorter account of Jesus’ temptation, Mark says “At once the Spirit sent him out into the desert.”

There is a lot in this which we will look at later, but for now notice that Jesus was loved then he was led. That is the way it needs to be. It is very easy for any of us to become stressed or anxious about God’s leading in our lives. We wonder what God wants us to do with our lives, we want some divine plan written out in the sky, but it will never happen like that. Personally, I think it is far more important to know God’s love, than to know God’s plans. Perhaps all the stress and anxiety around the whole area of Gods will for our lives is the result of us not having a deep enough experience of His love.

Prayer: Father thank you that it is the knowledge of your love that enables me to face the difficulties of life.
Bible in a year reading plan: - 2 Samuel: 8-10
